lessons.title: Flightpath monitoring and manual-flight readiness
lessons.concept: Pilots who consistently rely on an autopilot for flight path management may experience degraded ability to fly manually when required to do so.
lessons.explanation: For example, when flying in icing conditions, an automated system may make control inputs to compensate for ice accumulation. However, if the system exceeds its limits, it could disconnect and leave the aircraft in an undesired state. A pilot who has become unaccustomed to manual flight may not be prepared to handle the situation.

sections.title: Aeronautical Decision-Making in Flight
sections.summary: Apply continuous inflight risk reassessment, time-critical decisions and changing plans, continuation bias, diversion, and landing options, and the related limitations and risk controls.

lessons.title: Continuous inflight risk reassessment
lessons.concept: ffective risk management models utilize a closed-loop process.
lessons.explanation: The closed-loop nature of the 3P model requires a periodic check to verify successful mitigation of the risk. If the pilot perceives insufficient mitigation of that risk or detects a new hazard, the process and analysis resumes. Naturalistic Decision-Making Experienced pilots use naturalistic decision-making when the time available precludes a more formal analytical process.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how continuous inflight risk reassessment affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

practice.explanation: Recognize the cues for continuous inflight risk reassessment, apply the published procedure or limit, and reassess the result.

lessons.title: Time-critical decisions and changing plans
lessons.concept: Naturalistic Decision-Making Experienced pilots use naturalistic decision-making when the time available precludes a more formal analytical process.
lessons.explanation: In this type of scenario, pilots first assess whether the given situation strikes them as familiar. Rather than analyze the pros and cons of different actions, a pilot might start with a course of action that seems workable based on previously encountered patterns. In this type of decision-making, pilots may recall previous events and choose a course of action based on expectations.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how time-critical decisions and changing plans affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

lessons.title: Continuation bias, diversion, and landing options
lessons.concept: Process: The pilot considers the options available to mitigate the threat.
lessons.explanation: Choices may include: • Diverting to a nearby airport • Turning back, if conditions allow • Rerouting the flight to avoid the thunderstorms • Flying above the weather. During analytical decision-making, the pilot evaluates the pros and cons associated with each option and chooses one that should adequately reduce the level of risk. For example, the aircraft may not have the equipment or capability to fly above the weather or there might not be enough fuel on board for a significant reroute.
lessons.workedExample: Before committing to the next phase of flight, the pilot identifies how continuation bias, diversion, and landing options affects the aircraft and available margin, checks the handbook guidance, chooses a control, and names the condition that would trigger a delay, diversion, go-around, or landing.

glossary.definition: hapter 1: Introduction to Risk Management Introduction Pilots experience the joy of taking an aircraft and passengers on flights that are visually pleasing and productive.

glossary.term: Introduction to Risk Management operational margin
glossary.definition: The remaining safety capacity a pilot protects while applying introduction to risk management guidance.

glossary.term: Reviewing limits as proficiency and conditions change
glossary.definition: tep 5—Adjust for Specific Conditions Any flight a pilot makes involves an infinite combination of pilot skill, experience, conditions, and proficiency.
hapter 3: Identifying Hazards & Associated Risks Introduction The words “hazard” and “risk” seem simple, but these words are easily confused.

Using the PAVE Checklist to Identify Hazards As described in Chapter 1, the PAVE checklist provides a means to identify hazards using four convenient hazard “buckets.” Using all four checklist categories before a flight captures most hazards normally encountered.
To begin with, pilots should recognize hazards linked to causes of aircraft accidents as presented below: 1.
sing a Risk Assessment Matrix Once risk likelihood and severity have been determined, the pilot may use a tabular matrix to find the composite of these two parameters.

Risk Assessment Analysis The pilot, Tricia, identified nine separate hazards and associated risks that will affect a direct non-stop flight from Animas Air Park (00C) in Colorado to the Santa Rosa, California airport (KSTS).

Risk Assessment Analysis The pilot, Tricia, identified nine separate hazards and associated risks that will affect a direct non-stop flight from Animas Air Park (00C) in Colorado to the Santa Rosa, California airport (KSTS).
hapter 5: Mitigating Risk Introduction Risk mitigation reduces the likelihood or predicted severity of potential effects of hazards.

glossary.term: Controls for pilot, aircraft, and environment
glossary.definition: High risks the pilot cannot mitigate should lead to a no-go decision.

glossary.definition: roficiency A pilot who remains proficient is better prepared to defend against threats, errors, and undesired aircraft states.
ay find themselves in an unfamiliar situation that may result in serious errors.

Defenses against Threats, Errors, and Undesired Aircraft States When a pilot perceives a flight condition that requires attention, the pilot may know what to do immediately or may need time to process alternatives.
eliance on Automation The use of automation comes with certain cautions.
Figure 7-3 shows a typical flight mode annunciator displayed on the primary flight display (PFD) of a general aviation integrated flight deck.
Pilots who consistently rely on an autopilot for flight path management may experience degraded ability to fly manually when required to do so.
ffective risk management models utilize a closed-loop process.
Naturalistic Decision-Making Experienced pilots use naturalistic decision-making when the time available precludes a more formal analytical process.

glossary.definition: Process: The pilot considers the options available to mitigate the threat.
